speaker: interviewer
text: --June 21, 1995, Los Angeles, California, tape four. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Now you're coming to California. And tell a little bit about California.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I came home from-- from Miami to-- to New York, I said to my wife, we going to go to California-- to Los Angeles. And there you gonna feel better, because there, the climate is a nice climate. And I bought in time a new station wagon. And I packed in in the station wagon anything I could. I rented out-- I had my own house. The fact is, I had two houses. I had the-- I-- one cousin but remained alive, in our-- our family-- me with that cousin. He was taking care of it. And I packed in in the wagon everything I could. And then I send it by-- by-- by-- bus-- but go to California-- the other things what I couldn't take it. And we came to California. In California, I went-- my cousin was living-- while I was in New York-- was living in California. I went to him. I was staying with my wife. And my-- and my son and my daughter-- no, my daughter passed away in New York by soldiers. And we were staying there. And I was looking for something to do. So I saw an ad in the paper-- Zucky's, from Santa Monica. He-- he's looking for somebody to work. I went to him. He gave me the job. And I was working. But I didn't had experience in the deli. And he, after a while, he come over to me. And he said to me, you are very good worker. You are very good to the people. But the only thing is, you haven't got enough experience. He said, you go somewhere else and learn-- get more experience. My door will be always open for you whenever you want to come back. But learn more the business-- the delicatessen business-- at some other place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Lenny's on Beverly Drive. He needed somebody. I went to him to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, the same thing. He needed me, because the people were going for vacations. So he needed somebody to replace them. But I didn't have enough experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one old man went over to the boss. Not I-- I didn't-- I didn't hear that. But another worker heard that's what he said to him. Send him away, because he don't know. He has no experience. So one of the workers grabbed him by his throat. And she was-- he choked him. But the-- the people take her away from him. Why he wants to spoil my-- I have a family. And I have to make a living. And he don't want me to make a living. Why he has to go to the boss and send me away? He had no right to tell him that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was very important, that woman was. And he kept me further and longer. Then all the workers came back to work. So he didn't need me no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Junior's. And that time, I knew already. I had more-- more experience. I went to Junior's. And I was working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- and the people wanted me to wait on them. They didn't wanted the other workers to wait on them, because I polite and nice, and like I'm-- they're doing me a favor, not like I'm doing a favor. There were another guy, like, he was handling the customers like he was doing them a favor. But I didn't handle like that. So he became jealous at me. People by-- didn't want him to wait-- he didn't wait on nobody. But I had a line waiting for me-- I should wait on them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he got mad at me. And he start bossing me around. Go fill up the meat. Go fill up the cheeses. Go fill up the whole bakery goods. You know-- unable to send me away from the counter. I shouldn't be by the counter. He should only be the only one at the counter. But then-- and he's sitting down like this. And he watches me working. I said to him, Jack, aren't you getting paid, too? Why don't you do something, too? You're getting paid like me. So he went to the manager. And the manager came over to me. He said, if I wouldn't be afraid that you're gonna sue me, I would split your head-- just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when I heard that, I went into the boss. I said, I'm not going to work here, because the manager told me so and so. He said, don't worry, go back to the work. I will make them--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to move you along a little bit, Sam, because I think I would like you to tell a little bit then what happened in your jobs. But what-- you go onto-- to the next, but what life was like for you in California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- and what it was like for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I missed. But I was going with my car to California, from New York to California. We passed Albuquerque. My wife says to me, drive off from the freeway. She tells me, I'm doing it. I'm going down from the freeway-- next stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she grabs me, hugs me, kisses me. And I said, what is this for? She said, you took away all my pains. I have no more pains. Always, constantly, she had pains. And when-- when we passed Albuquerque, she stop feeling-- start feeling better. Anyway, we came to Los Angeles. And in the beginning, I came to Los Angeles. I didn't have no-- no apartment, no nothing, you know? And you got the jobs in the delicatessen. And you're at Lenny's.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these things happened. I got the apartment. I got an apartment. And I was working there. And my wife became sick. In '67 she passed away. She got it-- cancer on the liver. And she was by the doctors. And they-- and they took everything from her. She thought that they, you know, that how she feel, because she's not feeling not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you and your son are left together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Listen to this. And she passes away with-- from this. Because it was-- it was too late. When she went in to-- and they found out, it was already too late. And she passes away. She passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my son was going to school. And he was-- he was a-- he finished high school. He said he gonna go to work. He not going to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are working too hard, and I don't want you to work too hard. I want to go to work. I said, look. I want you-- you to sit down. We have a talk. I didn't have the opportunity to study. You have the opportunity. I'm giving you opportunity. Even I'm going to have to work two jobs, I'm going to work. And I'm going to support you. Go to school. If you're going to have here, you-- you're going to live good. You're going to remember. You gonna-- not going to worry about a job. Because jobs are going to beg for you when you're going to have here. And if you were going to have only high school, forget about jobs. You wouldn't-- you wouldn't have to work like I'm working to make a living. And he says to me, you're the best dad in the whole world. There cannot be a better person. And I'm so lucky to have you for my daddy. I'm going to listen to you. I'm going to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to the UCLA, took there college. And then he went to Chico-- Chino? Chino? To Chico-- Chico-- there, not far from the Sacramento. He went there for graduate-- undergraduate college or what they're calling it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, they called him out from Sacramento. He should come to work-- and too from Washington-- they called him to go to work. So he calls me up-- anytime he has any problem, or any good, any news--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's he do, Sam, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's working for the General Accounting Office. He's a manager there. And he has a very good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- he's not married yet. But I hope he's going to get married. I'm waiting for the day he should get married. Anyway, he has a very good job. He's very happy. And he calls me every day. Every time he has a problem, he calls me for I suggest what he should do. And I'm giving the advice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says to him, you're educated. I'm not. And you're asking me for advice? Yes, daddy, you are smarter than other people. This is what he tells me. And he takes my word, what I was saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I told him once, you can have a thousand friends, nobody could be as good like to you like I am. I can assure you for that. Because they ask-- they wanted something from you or they expect something from you. I'm your friend, because I'm your father. And I want the best for you without you paying me for it anything. I want to give you everything. Anyway, he knows that. And he always says to me, I'm lucky to have you for a father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your son's name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph Carmel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carmel, yeah. And he's very nice looking. He's tall-- very-- very good heart. Anyway, he asking me, Daddy, you need money? I will send you money. Don't worry. I have-- I have money. And I-- I didn't want it. I said, no, I-- my money's going to be yours anyway. I don't have to get from you. Anyway, so he went-- he went there to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're back. And-- and you're saying one of the things you taught your-- your own son was the importance of having an education.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I-- I wanted him to have education. He should be able to go on with nice life. Because for he not going to have education, he wouldn't have no good life for himself. I don't care what he's going to do. But he wouldn't have no-- this way, he can be anybody he wants to be if he has the education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what year, Sam-- you remarried, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remarried in-- in '80-- '83.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth-- Ash, she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she a survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's also a survivor from the Germans. She was in the camps-- the Germans, at Lagers. In Lagers she was. You know what I mean-- Lager?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was by the Germans. They send her from one place to the other. But she was at least with her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam, everything you went through-- you made many changes in your life. You survived a great deal. You-- you-- what would you think was that quality you had that enabled you to survive? What-- what is it, Sam, that you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have-- I-- I-- I cannot answer with my-- that question myself. But I'm wondering how did it come to me that I should go to Krakow, that I should go to Russia, I should go to Czechoslovakia, to-- what-- what lead-- led me to there? I don't know. Maybe my-- my mother-- she should rest in peace-- she was watching over me. She gave me that courage, that-- that knowledge.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I cannot answer myself. I'm telling you the honest true. But I did it on my own. And to put the money there, you know? I-- who would think about it? What do you think other things you did like that-- you put the money, you hid it on-- in your body. What else did you do, you think, that made it--

speaker: interviewee
text: I jumped on the trains. The Germans wouldn't catch me. I-- I-- I got away, because the-- that engineer saved me from the-- from that coal mine, because I would die there. I was stronger. I would live another few months. Because if you don't have food, enough to eat, and you have to work that hard, you cannot last forever and work so hard. And you're trying to make it in order you should get the bread. If you were making less than a month of work, what I had to give, I would get less bread. That's what the-- the punishment was if you're not working honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is something you'd like to leave to your son, some thoughts to children who are born after this about what you saw in the world? Is there anything you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They should be good to each other. And they should help each other, whatever they can, all the time. And they shouldn't hate nobody, because we are born the same way, all of the nationalities. We have different religions. This is like we have different parties-- a Democrat party, a Republican party, a Liberal party. We have all different kind of parties. And everybody wants it in his way. And-- and they don't come to-- to unity-- that they should work together. And we all should live better. We could live better. We have enough resources to live better. But this what takes-- the hating each other. And this makes the work worse. When we would love each other, and everybody would work together, we will have a better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my thought. This is my-- you understand? This is what I'm telling the people-- the-- for the people. They should know that they should, but they-- that they should go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Finished? [BACKGROUND NOISE] Sam, thank you. And thank you for all of the people who are going to listen to your story of survival from time on. Thank you, Sam.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: No touching.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kraindl Karmiol--

speaker: interviewer
text: Left to right-- you're starting left to right, Sam. And you're starting with--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left to right-- the further one-- the further one. The first three, I don't know. The-- the fourth is Kraindl Karmiol, Jonas Karmiol, Alta Zaltzman, Miriam Miadovnik, [NON-ENGLISH] Karmiol, and Harry Miadovnik.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in-- in the season picture-- this was maybe in '36-- 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: In where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pinczow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sara, Tobias, Alta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karmiol. Oh, they're-- wait. They're not Karmiol.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. And who's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Zaltzman is the second name, because she got married to Zaltzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: The middle picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The middle is Jonas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jonas who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonas Zaltzman, and Sarah Zaltzman, and Alta Zaltzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: In where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- this was taken in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: After they war, I didn't see them no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Karmiol, Szabsa Karmiol, and Miriam Miadovnik. And when was this picture taken, Sam?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Pinczow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pinczow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is-- who is your brother? What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szabsa Karmiol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No more. They was in the-- my brother and my sister-in-law, they were in the chambers. Miriam died here in-- in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's [NON-ENGLISH] Carmel, Sam Carmel, Sara Karmiol, Rachel-- Rachel--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, go on. Go to the next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Don't-- Don't touch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Michael-- Michael-- oye--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go to the next. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and his two children, one son and one daughter. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Babenhausen, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Say that that way, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I should call the names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Carmel, from the left to right, then it [NON-ENGLISH], then his wife-- I don't know the name-- [NON-ENGLISH] in the back. And the front is Carmel's wife-- I don't remember the name-- Carmel's daughter, Shirley, the little baby, and Carmel is in center. Then is Getzel-- and his wife-- his wife, Getzel. And this is George, in the bottom, the third one from the right. And then this is Carmel's two sons, David, and the other I don't remember the whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam, what's the family name in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Carmels. These are all Carmels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one do want to-- to point out-- who you remember the most?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carmel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you remember him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he came to Europe, and he helped the whole family-- not only me, but the whole family that was living. But he sended every six months by Passover and by Rosh Hoshanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was taken in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- do you know the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know the date, because I got it from them, this picture. [BACKGROUND NOISE] OK. Shirley Carmel-- she was four years old. And she came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that photograph taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is my daughter, my only daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did she-- she died when she was how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 16 and a 1/2 years old. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This has taken 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1966-- this is Sam Carmel. I had my own delicatessen store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: City and the state?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in Los Angeles. In the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: California.

speaker: interviewee
text: California.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Joseph Carmel. This is in Los Angeles, California. He was 18 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1956.
